problem: Let p be a prime andA_p = \{ k! \pmod{p} : 1≤ k<p\}.Is it true that| A_p| \sim (1-\tfrac{1}{e})p?
original_latex: Let $p$ be a prime and\[A_p = \{ k! \pmod{p} : 1\leq k<p\}.\]Is it true that\[\lvert A_p\rvert \sim (1-\tfrac{1}{e})p?\]
additional_context:
Since A_p/A_p=\{1,\ldots,p-1\} it follows that | A_p| \gg p^{1/2}. The best known lower bound is due to Grebennikov, Sagdeev, Semchankau, and Vasilevskii \cite{GSSV24},| A_p| ≥ (\sqrt{2}-o(1))p^{1/2},which follows from proving that | A_pA_p|=(1+o(1))p.

Wilson's theorem implies (p-2)!\equiv 1\pmod{p}, and hence | A_p|≤ p-2. It is open whether even | A_p|<p-2. This has been verified for all primes p<10^9 (see \cite{GSSV24}). Results on | A_p| on average were obtained by Klurman and Munsch \cite{KlMu17}.

In Hardy and Subbarao \cite{HaSu02} they raise the question, discussed in conversation with Erdős, of whether | A_p|=p-2 for many values of p. (This is also mentioned in problem A2 of Guy's collection.) Such a prime must be \equiv 1\pmod{4}. The answer is surely only finitely many (and probably only p=5, given the data mentioned above).

References

[GSSV24] Grebennikov, Alexandr and Sagdeev, Arsenii and Semchankau,
Aliaksei and Vasilevskii, Aliaksei, On the sequence {n! \bmod p}. Rev. Mat. Iberoam. (2024), 637--648.

[HaSu02] Hardy, G. E. and Subbarao, M. V., A modified problem of Pillai and some related questions. Amer. Math. Monthly (2002), 554--559.

[KlMu17] Klurman, Oleksiy and Munsch, Marc, Distribution of factorials modulo {p}. J. Th\'{e}or. Nombres Bordeaux (2017), 169--177.
reference_proof_hint:
No one knows at present.

Your statement is exactly a long‑standing conjecture (usually attributed to Stauduhar, and also appearing in Guy’s *Unsolved Problems in Number Theory*): if
[
h(p):=|\\{1!,2!,\dots,(p-1)!\\}|\pmod p=\lvert A_p\rvert,
]
then
[
\frac{h(p)}{p}\ \longrightarrow\ 1-\frac1e\qquad (p\to\infty).
]
This is stated explicitly as **Stauduhar’s Conjecture** in recent work.  It is also recorded as a conjectured asymptotic in the literature on factorials mod $p$, with the remark that “very little seems to be known” about (V(0,p-1)=h(p)) beyond partial bounds. ([Journal de Théorie des Nombres][1])

## Why ((1-1/e)p) is the “natural” guess

Heuristically, the residues (k!\bmod p) “look random” as $p$ grows. A common strengthening is a **Poisson$1$ occupancy** heuristic: for each fixed (k\ge 0), the proportion of residue classes hit *exactly* $k$ times by ({1!,\dots,(p-1)!}) should tend to (\frac{1}{e,k!}). 

If the multiplicities are asymptotically Poisson with mean $1$, then
